title: The Knowledge Economy Is Over. Welcome to the Allocation Economy.
executive_summary: Shipper argues that AI is ending the knowledge economy — the era where professionals are paid for what they know — and replacing it with the allocation economy, where professionals are paid for how well they direct, evaluate, and manage AI systems that do the knowing

The structural shift is universal: even junior employees will need to develop skills previously reserved for the 12% of the workforce in formal management roles — directing work, evaluating quality, and allocating resources
AI acts as an abstraction layer over lower-level thinking — the same way cloud computing abstracted server management, AI is now abstracting the execution of knowledge work itself

For wealth management, the implication is direct: advisors whose value is rooted in holding information will be disintermediated; advisors whose value is rooted in judgment, relationships, and directing AI will compound their advantage
The essay was published in January 2024 and updated in March 2026 — the original thesis has only been accelerated by the pace of AI capability development since
the_one_insight: You will not be judged on how much you know, but on how well you can allocate and manage resources to get work done. Every knowledge worker is becoming a manager of AI. The question is whether they are ready.

key_quotes.text: In the age of AI, every maker becomes a manager.
key_quotes.context: On the universal shift in how professional value is created and measured

key_quotes.text: You won't be judged on how much you know, but instead on how well you can allocate and manage the resources to get work done.
key_quotes.context: On the core competency of the allocation economy

key_quotes.text: AI is an abstraction layer over lower-level thinking.
key_quotes.context: On the structural mechanism by which AI changes the nature of knowledge work
